## [![](https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1991296?s=64&v=4)\ ggerganov](https://github.com/ggerganov) [on Nov 2, 2025Nov 2, 2025](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/discussions/16938\#discussion-9097938)   Maintainer

|     |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- |
| ## Overview

This guide highlights the key features of the new [SvelteKit-based WebUI](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/pull/14839) of `llama.cpp`.

The new WebUI in combination with the advanced backend capabilities of the [llama-server](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/tree/master/tools/server) delivers the ultimate local AI chat experience. A few characteristics that set this project ahead of the alternatives:

- Free, open-source and community-driven
- Excellent performance on all hardware
- Advanced context and prefix caching
- Parallel and remote user support
- Extremely lightweight and memory efficient
- Vibrant and creative community
- 100% privacy

## Getting started

1. Get llama.cpp: [Install](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/blob/master/docs/install.md) \| [Download](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/releases) \| [Build](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/blob/master/docs/build.md)

2. Start the `llama-server` tool:



```
# sample server running gpt-oss-20b at http://127.0.0.1:8033

llama-server -hf ggml-org/gpt-oss-20b-GGUF --jinja -c 0 --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8033
```

3. Open and start using the WebUI in your browser:


![image](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/1991296/509358451-bdb4371a-14e4-47ce-b875-1e422bf8a27d.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..hEgQvhXHSysmSgo0Qgoh50N0f2NhOxmu6z8i3e27ROk)

Tip

For a simple, GUI-based setup of `llama.cpp` on Mac, try the new [LlamaBarn](https://github.com/ggml-org/LlamaBarn) application

## Features

The new WebUI is packed with many useful features to enhance your local AI experience. Following are a few examples.

### Text document processing

Add multiple text files from disk or from the clipboard to the context of your conversation:

![image](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/1991296/509358979-198776ed-c717-4305-b1ba-a3eed6914ed7.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..y7dbv5vX97ToVUVNBmtobFmSva_lF_Nr0ycktaHvGMw)

### PDF document processing

Attach one or multiple PDFs to your conversation. By default, the contents of the PDFs will be converted to RAW text, excluding any visuals.

![image](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/1991296/508711598-b0d4ad37-6dcc-4fb9-bddb-0064d354bd40.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..fHHxIfPGemWKg6ftq3hBwgWmEXZI-NJ0M1NgppGfIpM)

Optionally, the WebUI can process the PDFs as images when the AI model supports it.

|     |     |
| --- | --- |
| ![image](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/1991296/509386271-fd789263-b424-4258-80c2-e7662feeaade.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..nLL5cPMbtoBCz7QJPG1IGUdrBZzx6YUyDbadY0NXwQ4) | ![image](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/1991296/508711613-ab36d33a-e25a-4a7f-8188-61ab7b38c021.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..sYwTmj6td30Z3AhWp6RirbzegF3jXX1t6C1TYMyCI8c) |

### Image inputs

When the selected AI model has vision input capabilities, the WebUI allows you to insert images into your conversation:

![image](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/1991296/508716894-80396004-d716-43b8-8a21-76e5cfdf301c.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..gBnbdxEseMuKNx7vDTWf6MrdD2Mw8x8cUfg8CuS_xZg)

Images can be inserted in addition to a textual context:

![image](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/1991296/508711632-b2305355-7bf5-4edb-970c-0632349897fb.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.._OwFwxzyz5wkOL47z54BkTp6bTdQcT-AUTUYlyPde9U)

### Conversation branching

Branch from previous points of the conversation by editing or regenerating a message:

webui-edits-0-thumb-small.mp4

### Parallel conversations

Run multiple chat conversations at the same time:

webui-parallel-0-thumb-small.mp4

Parallel image processing is also supported:

webui-parallel-1-thumb-small.mp4

### Override default sampling parameters

Start the `llama-server` using a set of default sampling parameters:

```
# set the default Top-K to be 5 and the default Temperature to be 0.80

llama-server -hf ggml-org/gpt-oss-120b-GGUF --jinja -c 0 --port 8033 --alias gpt-oss-120b --top-k 5 --temp 0.80
```

These parameters will now become the default values in the WebUI settings:

webui-parameters-0-thumb-small.mp4

More info: [#16515](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/pull/16515)

### Render math expressions

The WebUI can render mathematical expressions:

![image](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/1991296/508717406-74508f60-bfcc-438d-90ef-4c5aa208cd3d.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..FmGrx-O_hWIlbtHC82p4CEZwdPgUNxC4u5XqlOdorTA)

### Input via URL parameters

The WebUI supports passing input through the URL parameters:

webui-url-input-0-thumb-small.mp4

### HTML/JS preview

The WebUI supports inline rendering of generated HTML/JS code:

webui-js-0-thumb-small.mp4

More info: [#16757](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/pull/16757)

### Constrained generation

Specify a custom JSON schema to constrain the generated output to a specific format. As an example, here is generic invoice data extraction from multiple documents:

webui-constrained-0-thumb-small.mp4

### Import/Export

Use the Import/Export options to manage your private conversations directly through the WebUI:

|     |     |
| --- | --- |
| ![image](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/1991296/509342041-a107f82d-15be-402e-ab0e-32ca8a340dc9.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..aAoe8n_N8O5chfwQPBQ3zYh_ZBwDEprRH-B3j8-TlYI) | ![image](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/1991296/509342123-e3dc8ebb-c653-49a1-b852-608a5cc75b6f.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..My8If9pTYEG9hHW0INXz7i35ArLalcRp1us0k_R25_o) |

### Efficient SSM context management

The context management and prefix caching of State Space Models (SSMs, e.g. Mamba) can be tricky. `llama-server` solves this problem efficiently for one or multiple users with minimum reprocessing.

Here is an example of context branching using a hybrid LLM:

webui-ssm-0-thumb-small.mp4

### Mobile compatibility

The new WebUI is mobile friendly:

|     |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- |
| ![IMG_2153](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/1991296/509338797-f1634f32-64ed-4dde-998b-89136f203af7.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..qbQyh6Kfr0KemSX3YBYAhegFpDUyx4JF5tj_zFhdga8) | ![IMG_2154](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/1991296/509338928-6b9400af-e3e9-42a3-9789-c0b936c7e89f.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..Tk_3dFx4yAq92fQPO_FvLQQhW7nR_sZ-CU7yEqPAC4k) | ![IMG_2149](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/1991296/509329805-09e5b747-05dc-4551-ad0b-f1b703938465.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..W3SE2Bbq676t-ZGl3PH3LNDOqZFpn3TwI7QHV0ZfUq4) |

## Sample commands

A few `llama-server` commands used for the examples above:

```
# lightweight, gpt-oss-20b
llama-server --jinja -c 0 --port 8033 -hf ggml-org/gpt-oss-20b-GGUF --alias "gpt-oss-20b"

# text-only, gpt-oss-120b with greedy sampling by default
llama-server --jinja -c 0 --port 8033 -hf ggml-org/gpt-oss-120b-GGUF --alias "gpt-oss-120b" --top-k 1

# vision-enabled, Qwen3 VL 30B A3B, accessible from the local network
llama-server --jinja -c 0 --port 8033 -hf ggml-org/Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Instruct-Q8_0-GGUF --alias "Qwen3 VL 30B A3B" --host 192.168.100.3

# hybrid, Granite 4.0 H Small with 1 million tokens context
llama-server --jinja -c 0 --port 8033 -hf ggml-org/granite-4.0-h-small-Q8_0-GGUF --alias "Granite 4.0 Hybrid Small"
```

| The llama.cpp server web UI does support date/time template variables — but the syntax is different from what you tried. The correct variables are:<br>- `current_date` → replaced with `YYYY-MM-DD` (UTC)<br>- `current_time` → replaced with `HH:MM:SS` (UTC)<br>- `current_timestamp` → replaced with a full UTC timestamp like `2025-05-08 22:19:33` [Substack](https://simonw.substack.com/p/trying-out-llamacpps-new-vision-support)<br>So in your system prompt, use something like:<br>```<br>Today's date is {{current_date}}. You are a helpful assistant...<br>```<br>or just the bare variable without braces — the exact delimiter syntax can vary by build, so try both `current_date` and `{{current_date}}` if one doesn't work. The `{CURRENT_DATE}` you saw is likely the Open WebUI convention (which uses `{{CURRENT_DATE}}`), not llama.cpp's native web UI.<br>One caveat: these use UTC, so if your local timezone matters, you may want to hardcode the offset or just manually note your timezone in the system prompt. [Substack](https://simonw.substack.com/p/trying-out-llamacpps-new-vision-support) |

[Chat with Canvas](https://ui.llamaindex.ai/demo/canvas)

[Mermaid Diagram](https://ui.llamaindex.ai/demo/mermaid)

## Quick Start

Add a chatbot to your project with Shadcn CLI:

`npx shadcn@latest add https://ui.llamaindex.ai/r/chat.json`

or

Install manually:

`npm install @llamaindex/chat-ui`

# Chat Architecture with Open WebUI, llama.cpp, and Phi

[![Muneeb Ahmad](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:32:32/1*IF1X_T0YzkAeJ-b5CHawgw.jpeg)](https://muneebsa.medium.com/?source=post_page---byline--26b7928bd62c---------------------------------------)

[Muneeb Ahmad](https://muneebsa.medium.com/?source=post_page---byline--26b7928bd62c---------------------------------------)

·

Aug 15, 2025

5

[Listen](https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2Fplans%3Fdimension%3Dpost_audio_button%26postId%3D26b7928bd62c&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fmuneebsa.medium.com%2Fchat-architecture-with-open-webui-llama-cpp-and-phi-26b7928bd62c&source=---header_actions--26b7928bd62c---------------------post_audio_button------------------)

Share

**Why this article matters**: By completion, you will understand more than basic chat functionality by comprehending the complete technical pipeline from **user interface** through **HTTP requests** to **runtime** execution, **model** processing, and **hardware** utilization. This comprehensive knowledge of the **UI** → **HTTP** → **model server** (runtime) → **model** → **CPU**/RAM data flow enables effective optimization of local language model deployments across diverse implementation scenarios.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*2PlgQ07HxKi6lmQJKXwa3Q.png)

## What you’ll build

This implementation demonstrates a complete local AI chat system using open-source components. Each layer serves a specific purpose in the processing pipeline.

### **Core Architecture**

- **UI**: Open WebUI (installed via `pip`)
- **Runtime (Model Server)**: `llama.cpp` in server mode (OpenAI-compatible API)
- **Model**: Phi-4-mini-instruct-GGUF (Q4\_K\_M, GGUF), CPU-only
- **OS**: Windows + macOS (Intel & Apple Silicon)

This stack provides enterprise-grade functionality while remaining accessible for development and small-scale deployment. The combination ensures reliable performance across different hardware configurations.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*AU32r1IbiqM0ORvlIY7dSw.png)

Chat Stack: End-to-End Flow from UI to Model Execution

## **Core Concepts**

Understanding these fundamental principles enables effective deployment and optimization of local language models. Each concept directly impacts system performance, response quality, and resource utilization.

### **Text Processing & Model Input**

**Tokens & tokenization (model input format)** Text undergoes conversion to integer sequences via tokenization. Models process only tokens, not raw text. Token efficiency directly correlates with reduced latency and computational cost in CPU-bound environments.

**Context window & truncation (memory constraints)** Phi-4 Mini supports approximately **128K** tokens spanning system prompts, conversation history, and generated output. Exceeding this limit triggers automatic truncation of earlier content. Strategic prompt design prevents information loss.

**Prompt roles & templates (message formatting)** OpenAI-style message structures (`system`, `user`, `assistant`) convert to unified prompt strings through chat templates. The runtime handles this transformation automatically, ensuring model compatibility.

### **Generation Control & Output Quality**

**Sampling controls (creativity parameters)** Generation parameters function as precision controls:

- **Temperature**: Higher values increase output diversity; lower values enhance determinism
- **Top\_p/top\_k**: Token filtering mechanisms that prevent incoherent responses. Think of these as “word choice filters” that limit which words the model considers at each step. Top\_k says “only pick from the best 40 words,” while top\_p says “only consider words that make up 90% of the probability.” Both prevent the model from choosing unlikely or nonsensical words that could derail the response.

**Stop sequences & output limits** Stop tokens (such as `"</s>"`) ensure clean generation termination. Maximum token limits prevent infinite responses and maintain resource control.

**Safety & system prompts (behavioral guidance)** Smaller models require explicit instruction for optimal performance. Concise system prompts defining role, tone, and constraints significantly improve output quality and consistency.

### **Technical Implementation**

**Quantization fundamentals (GGUF, Q4\_K\_M)** GGUF provides an optimized file format for local inference. **Q4\_K\_M** quantization reduces memory requirements to 4 bits per weight while preserving quality. Think of it like image compression: **Q4** means each piece of model data uses only **4 bits** instead of the original 16 bits. **K** refers to the specific **compression algorithm** used. **M** stands for “ **medium**” quality setting, balancing file size with performance. This configuration suits 16GB systems effectively.

**Server architecture (OpenAI compatibility)** llama.cpp exposes standard `/v1/chat/completions` endpoints. OpenAI JSON protocol compatibility enables runtime substitution without client code modification.

**CPU parallelism (performance optimization)** Thread count adjustments can enhance generation speed by utilizing multiple CPU cores simultaneously during model inference. This optimization occurs at the CPU/RAM hardware layer of the chat architecture, where the actual mathematical computations for token generation take place. While the llama.cpp runtime manages thread allocation, the parallel processing happens at the lowest level where model weights are loaded into memory and matrix operations are executed across available CPU cores. Default configurations typically provide adequate performance for development and testing scenarios.

These concepts form the theoretical foundation for practical implementation. Mastering them enables informed troubleshooting and system optimization decisions.

## Why these tools and frameworks?

Tool selection significantly impacts deployment success, maintenance overhead, and scalability potential. This comparison evaluates options across three critical dimensions: **user interface accessibility**, **runtime performance characteristics**, and **model compatibility requirements**.

Each category presents multiple viable alternatives with distinct trade-offs. The recommendations balance ease of implementation against long-term flexibility and resource efficiency. Understanding these choices enables informed decisions based on specific project constraints and technical requirements.

Key Local Chat Stack Options: UI Layers, Runtimes, and CPU-Friendly Models

### **Final Selection Rationale**

The recommended stack combines **Open WebUI**, **llama.cpp**, and **Phi-4-Mini-Instruct** based on three primary selection criteria: deployment simplicity, resource efficiency, and ecosystem compatibility.

**Open WebUI** emerges as the optimal interface choice due to its straightforward pip installation process and native OpenAI API integration. Unlike desktop applications that require additional setup steps, Open WebUI provides immediate web-based access while maintaining professional-grade features. The built-in RAG capabilities offer natural expansion paths for document-based applications.

**llama.cpp** serves as the (model server) runtime foundation for its exceptional CPU optimization and minimal resource footprint. The built-in OpenAI-compatible server mode eliminates integration complexity while delivering production-ready performance. This choice ensures broad hardware compatibility without requiring specialized GPU infrastructure.

**Phi-4-Mini-Instruct** provides the best quality-to-resource ratio for CPU deployment scenarios. Microsoft’s official GGUF distribution guarantees compatibility and optimization quality. The 2.2GB Q4\_K\_M quantization fits comfortably within typical development machine constraints while maintaining conversation coherence.

This combination prioritizes **learning value** and **practical deployment** over maximum performance. Each component exposes clear configuration options and debugging interfaces, enabling systematic understanding of the complete inference pipeline. The stack scales naturally from development through small production deployments.

## **Hands-on: End-to-End Implementation**

This implementation section demonstrates complete system assembly from component installation through functional validation. Each step builds upon previous configurations, resulting in a fully operational local chat architecture. The process emphasizes reproducibility and systematic verification of component integration.

### Implementation Overview

The deployment follows a logical sequence that ensures each component can be tested independently before integration:

- Download and position the language model in the designated directory structure
- Install and configure the llama.cpp inference server with API endpoint validation
- Deploy the Open WebUI interface and establish communication with the server
- Verify end-to-end functionality through comprehensive system testing

### Detailed Implementation Steps

**1) System Prerequisites**

- **Hardware**: 16 GB RAM recommended; SSD storage; CPU-only processing
- **Network Ports**: Open WebUI (8080), llama.cpp server (8000)
- **Python Environment**: Version 3.11 for Open WebUI compatibility

**2) Model Acquisition and Deployment**

Create the project directory structure and obtain the quantized model:

**Directory Setup:**

Create the necessary folders for models and llama

```
# Windows
mkdir C:\my_projects\chat\models
mkdir C:\my_projects\chat\llama\bin

# macOS
mkdir -p ~/my_projects/chat/models
mkdir -p ~/my_projects/chat/llama/bin
```

**Model Download:** Obtain the official GGUF model from `microsoft/Phi-4-mini-instruct-gguf`:

- **Model home page**: [link](https://huggingface.co/microsoft/Phi-4-mini-instruct)
- **Download model**: Several community contributors (bartowski, Mungert) have successfully created GGUF versions that work with llama.cpp for CPU inference. Follow the below steps:

\- Visit [this page](https://huggingface.co/bartowski/microsoft_Phi-4-mini-instruct-GGUF)

\- Locate the link for “Phi-4-mini-instruct-Q4\_K\_M.gguf” and click it.

\- Download the file on the next page.

\- Move the file to the destination folder
- **Downloaded File**: Phi-4-mini-instruct-Q4\_K\_M.gguf
- **Destination**:
- `C:\my_projects\chat\models\` (Windows)
- `~/my_projects/chat/models/` (macOS)

**3) llama.cpp Server Installation and Configuration**

**Download Instructions:**

\- Visit [this page](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/releases)

\- Download the ZIP file for your operating system and hardware architecture

\- Extract the ZIP file and move its executable and supported files into the ‘bin’ folder of below destination folder.

\- **Windows**: `C:\my_projects\chat\llama\` **\- macOS**: `~/my_projects/chat/llama/`

**Server Initialization:**

**Windows:**

```
cd C:\my_projects\chat\llama\bin
llama-server.exe -m C:\my_projects\chat\models\microsoft_Phi-4-mini-instruct-Q4_K_M.gguf --port 8000
```

**macOS:**

```
cd ~/my_projects/chat/llama/bin
./llama-server -m ~/my_projects/chat/models/microsoft_Phi-4-mini-instruct-Q4_K_M.gguf --port 8000
```

The server exposes OpenAI-compatible endpoints at `http://127.0.0.1:8000/v1/chat/completions`. The `--alias` parameter standardizes the model identifier for client requests.

**API Endpoint Validation:**

**Windows:**

```
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:8000/v1/chat/completions ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer sk-local" ^
  -d "{\"model\":\"phi3-mini-4k-q4\",\"messages\":[{\"role\":\"system\",\"content\":\"You are concise and helpful.\"},{\"role\":\"user\",\"content\":\"What is the capital of France\"}],\"stream\":false}"
```

**macOS:**

```
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:8000/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer sk-local" \
  -d '{"model":"phi3-mini-4k-q4","messages":[{"role":"system","content":"You are concise and helpful."},{"role":"user","content":"What is the capital of France."}],"stream":false}'
```

A successful JSON response confirms server functionality and model loading.

**4) Open WebUI Installation and Integration**

**Pre-requisite**

Make sure Python 3.11 and above is installed

```
##### Windows =============================

# 0) Make sure Python 3.11 is installed
#    Download from https://www.python.org/downloads/release/python-3110/
#    During installation: ✅ check "Add Python to PATH"

# 1) Create the venv using Python 3.11
cd %USERPROFILE%
python -m venv openwebui

# 2) Activate it
openwebui\Scripts\activate

# 3) Sanity check: should say Python 3.11.x
python --version

##### Mac =============================

# 0) Make sure 3.11 is installed
brew install python@3.11

# 1) Leave the current venv and delete it
deactivate 2>/dev/null || true
rm -rf ~/venvs/openwebui

# 2) Recreate the venv using Homebrew’s 3.11 interpreter
"$(brew --prefix python@3.11)"/bin/python3.11 -m venv ~/venvs/openwebui

# 3) Activate it
source ~/venvs/openwebui/bin/activate

# 4) Sanity check: should say Python 3.11.x
python -V
```

**Universal Installation (All Platforms):**

```
pip install open-webui
open-webui serve

# Please note that on slower Windows machine the 'pip install' command
# may take many minutes so please be patient.
```

**Windows Multi-Python Environment:**

py -3.11 -m pip install open-webui

open\_webui serve

Access the interface at [**http://localhost:8000**](http://localhost:8000/). and test the complete system by sending a message through the Open WebUI chat interface. Successful response generation confirms proper **UI → HTTP → server → model** data flow and OpenAI protocol compliance.

### Implementation Summary

The deployment process establishes a complete local chat architecture through systematic component integration. The model serves as the foundation, providing the intelligence layer. The llama.cpp server creates the runtime environment with OpenAI-compatible APIs. Open WebUI delivers the user interface with enterprise-grade functionality.

This architecture validates each component independently before integration, ensuring reliable troubleshooting and maintenance. The resulting system provides privacy-focused AI capabilities without external dependencies, suitable for development, testing, and production deployment scenarios.

## **Appendix — Technical Glossary**

This reference section defines key terminology essential for understanding local language model deployment and optimization. Terms are organized by functional category to facilitate systematic comprehension and practical application.

**File Formats & Storage**

- **GGUF** — An optimized binary format for local language model storage and inference. Designed specifically for efficient loading and execution in CPU-based environments like llama.cpp.
- **Quantization (Q4\_K\_M)** — A compression technique that reduces model weights from 16-bit to 4-bit precision. Achieves significant memory reduction (approximately 75%) while maintaining acceptable response quality for most applications.

**Text Processing & Model Interface**

- **Token** — The fundamental unit of text processing for language models. All input text undergoes tokenization before model processing, with efficiency directly impacting computational performance.
- **Context Window (128** K **)** — The maximum token capacity for active model memory, encompassing system prompts, conversation history, and generated output.
- **Stop Sequences** — Predefined text patterns that signal generation completion. Examples include end-of-turn markers and special tokens that ensure clean response termination.

**Generation Control & Optimization**

- **Sampling** — The algorithmic process for selecting subsequent tokens during text generation. Primary parameters include temperature (creativity control), top\_p (probability filtering), and top\_k (candidate limitation).
- **Alias** — A user-defined identifier for model instances exposed through the server API. Enables consistent client-server communication regardless of underlying file naming conventions (e.g., `phi3-mini-4k-q4`).
